topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The news landed in my lap like a damp, unremarkable piece of paper. Liam had moved. Across the country. I stared at the glossy brochure for the new apartment complex, tracing the outline of a balcony with my fingertip. A faint pressure settled behind my eyes, a dull ache that didn't demand attention. I picked up my phone, intending to text him, but the words caught in my throat. It felt like a small, insignificant ripple in a vast, placid lake. I scrolled through old photos, the smiles frozen in time, and then put the phone down, the screen’s light reflecting off my unreadable expression. It was just a fact now, a change in geography, nothing more substantial than a shift in coordinates.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually leaving?" I asked, the question sounding flat, almost rehearsed. Maya was packing boxes in our shared living room, the sound of tape ripping echoing in the sudden silence. She didn't look up immediately, methodically folding a sweater. I watched the dust motes dance in the afternoon sun slanting through the window, noticing how utterly unperturbed I was by the seismic shift happening in our friendship. My hands were resting loosely on the arm of the sofa, the fabric yielding beneath my weight without any real engagement. I offered a small nod when she finally looked up, my gaze meeting hers briefly before drifting back to the pattern on the rug.

[story 3]

I spent an hour staring at the wall, the beige paint seeming to absorb all available energy. When Chloe finally brought up the move, it was delivered in a clipped, matter-of-fact tone, as if announcing the weather forecast. "He's heading to Seattle," she stated, not seeking a reaction. I blinked slowly, processing the information with a kind of detached observation. There was no surge of panic, no sudden yearning; just a steady, low hum of acceptance. I picked up a book from the shelf, flipping through the pages without truly absorbing the text. The weight of the impending distance felt less like a loss and more like an environmental adjustment I didn't particularly care to manage.
